original_abstract:
Hydrogen fuel cells represent a cornerstone for the next generation of zero-emission automotive transportation. Central to their advancement is the development of electrocatalysts that are efficient, durable, and cost-effective enough to overcome the sluggish kinetics of the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). In this study, we report the synthesis of metal-organic framework (MOF)-derived non-precious dual-metal single-atom catalysts (Zn/Co-N-C), featuring isolated Co-N₄ and Zn-N₄ local structures. 

These catalysts demonstrate exceptional ORR activity with a half-wave potential ($E_{1/2}$) of 0.938 V vs. RHE and remarkable durability, showing a minimal shift ($\Delta E_{1/2} = -8.5$ mV) after 50,000 electrochemical cycles. The practical utility of this material was confirmed under realistic fuel cell operating conditions, where it achieved a record-breaking peak power density of $\sim$1 W cm⁻²—the highest reported to date for single-atom catalysts in alkaline fuel cells.

To gain structural and mechanistic insights, we employed *operando* X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS). Our findings identify the Co atom within the Co-N₄ architecture as the primary catalytic center, which binds an axial oxygenated species to form an $O_{ads}$-Co-N₄ moiety during the reaction. These experimental observations were supported by theoretical studies using potential-dependent microkinetic modeling and core-level shift calculations. The computational results align closely with our experimental data, elucidating the specific bonding behavior of oxygen species on Co-N₄ centers. This work establishes a comprehensive understanding of the active sites in Zn/Co-N-C catalysts and provides a strategic framework for the future design of high-performance, single-site electrocatalysts for energy conversion technologies.
humanized_abstract:
Introduction: Hydrogen fuel cells are likely to be an important technology for next generation of “green” vehicles. However, the electrocatalysts need to be improved in terms of activity, stability and cost for reducing the large overpotential of the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). Results: Herein, Zn/Co-N-C materials as a kind of metal-organic framework (MOF)-derived non-precious dual-metal single-atom catalysts were fabricated, and demonstrated the dispersed Co-N4 and Zn-N4 active sites at the atomic scale.

IrPc-based single-atom catalysts exhibit unprecedented performance as the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) catalyst. The half-wave potential ($E_{1/2}$) value for the ORR is recorded at 0.938 V vs RHE. No significant shift in $E_{1/2}$ value is observed after undergoing 50,000 cycle durability tests ($\Delta E_{1/2} = -8.5$ mV). The effectiveness of this catalyst material is verified during fuel cell operation at working conditions, where this catalyst records the highest peak power density of $\sim$1 W cm⁻².

To understand their structural and mechanistic properties, *operando* X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) is used in this study. By investigating the properties of Zn/Co-N-C catalysts, the active sites on the surface are revealed. The Co atom in the Co-N4 structure is confirmed to be the active catalytic sites where axial oxygenated species are adsorbed as an Oads-Co-N4 species during the reaction. By combining potential-dependent microkinetic modeling and core-level shift calculations at the atomistic level, the studies confirm the experimental results and elucidate the bonding nature of oxygen species over the Co-N4 sites. This study gives a complete understanding of the active sites in Zn/Co-N-C catalysts and gives a guidance for the design of new single-site catalysts for different energy related applications.

document.input:
Hydrogen fuel cells represent a cornerstone for the next generation of zero-emission automotive transportation.
